checks.a_analysis: The output contains a heading and a table that accurately reflect text found in the source document. There is no evidence of hallucinatory or invented text, such as made-up introductions or section labels not derived from the image.
The source document does not contain any contact information (phone numbers, emails, addresses, or URLs), and neither does the output. No fabrication has occurred.

The source is a single-page document. The output captures the primary title and the legend data. While significant amounts of text within the image are missing, the document structure as a whole is present.

The output interprets the 'Legend' portion of the graphic as a table. The legend contains 10 distinct text items (arranged in two columns). The output table has 5 rows and two columns (except the last row), capturing all legend items correctly without loss of rows.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The output fails to capture a significant amount of textual information present in the source image. Missing items include the top banner text ('NC DEPARTMENT OF PUBLIC SAFETY', 'Emergency Management'), the map's internal title and date ('Risk Building Footprint Refresh', 'Current Status 6/16/2025'), and the metadata footer ('Scale: 1:3,179,372', '1 inch equals 50 miles', 'Spatial Reference Name: NAD 1983 StatePlane North Carolina FIPS 3200 Feet', 'Status as of: 6/16/2025'). Collectively, these missing text blocks constitute more than 25% of the slide's unique textual content.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source contains the following text: 'Risk Building Footprint Refresh', 'Current Status 6/16/2025', 'Scale: 1:3,179,372', '1 inch equals 50 miles', 'Spatial Reference Name: NAD 1983 StatePlane North Carolina FIPS 3200 Feet', 'Status as of: 6/16/2025', 'NC DEPARTMENT OF PUBLIC SAFETY', 'Emergency Management'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The text identified in the source is entirely absent from the JSON elements.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text that was successfully captured (the main heading and the legend) is accurate and contains no meaning-altering errors.

checks.a_analysis: The output does not contain any duplicated text blocks. Each major section of the source (headings, legend text) appears exactly once in the structured JSON.

The output contains a single Level 1 heading 'Building Footprint Update Status', which corresponds to the main title of the page. No other headings are defined, and there are no instances of skipped levels or incorrect nesting.

The legend section of the source document consists of two independent columns of information: one for Project Phases (left) and one for Status Types (right). The conversion process forced this visual layout into a single data table, creating arbitrary row-wise pairings (e.g., 'Phase 1 (2020)' paired with 'In Progress'). In the source, these items are not semantically linked in a row-based relationship; they are separate legend categories. This structural corruption misrepresents the logic of the legend.
checks.b_evidence_source: The legend in the PDF has two separate columns: Left column lists Phases 1-4 and 'Not Started'; Right column lists status states like 'In Progress' and 'Initial QC Complete'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The JSON table (element index 1) pairs these into rows: ["Phase 1 (2020)", "In Progress"], ["Phase 2 (2021)", "Initial QC Complete"], etc.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order in the output generally follows the source, though it moves the Legend (Table) before the Map (Image). This is a common adjustment for accessibility to provide context before a complex image and is considered an acceptable change.
There are no traditional bulleted or numbered lists in the source document. The legend, which might have been a candidate for a list, was converted into a table (noted as a structural defect in S3).
There are no hyperlinks present in either the source PDF or the output JSON.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML document faithfully reproduces the visual organization of the source PDF. The title is at the top, followed by the map legend (converted into an accessible table), the map image itself, and then the various organizational logos. While the logos are repositioned from the corners of the page to a vertical stack at the bottom, this is standard responsive HTML behavior and maintains the visual flow and relationship of information. The map content and its internal elements (counties, numbers, internal labels) are correctly preserved within the map image.

The map legend from the source PDF has been converted into a 'Table: Legend' in the HTML. This table is correctly rendered with clear borders, distinct headers, and aligned columns. It accurately captures the text from the legend (Phases and Statuses) in a legible format. There are no overlapping cells or misaligned columns.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML. The primary map graphic ('Risk Building Footprint Refresh') is rendered clearly. The agency logos (DPS Emergency Management, EMAP Accredited, and NC Emergency Management) are also present as standalone images. All images have correctly rendered and are visible in the screenshots.

There is no significant duplication of body content, paragraphs, or tables. The title and legend text are represented once in the document flow. While the triangle logo appears both within the map image and as a standalone image below it, and the map title appears as both a caption and within the image itself, these are standard accessible document practices (providing text/image alternatives for visual-only content) and do not constitute a defect in visual fidelity.
